ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for carrot-orange juice blend; closest parent is generic fruit juice (liquid) FOODON_03301710. The carrot component is a vegetable juice but the blend is categorised under fruit juice as the primary flavour profile. Match type set to closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fruit and vegetable juices as a category; no specific code exists for carrot-orange blend in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Match type set to closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for carrot-orange juice blend in the published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of carrot orange juice is approximately 300 g (300 ml), providing around 120 kcal, 28 g carbohydrate, and 80 mg vitamin C per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Carrot juice SR Legacy (FDC ID 167771) and Orange juice SR Legacy (FDC ID 169098), composite 50:50 blend; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database for regional validation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a carrot-orange juice blend. Values are derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for carrot juice (FDC ID 167771) and orange juice (FDC ID 169098) blended in an approximate 50:50 ratio by weight, consistent with the input record's per-100g profile (energy 40 kcal, protein 0.67 g, fat 0.17 g, carbohydrate 9.33 g). The input values are plausible and consistent with this composite. HPB Singapore Foodo was consulted for regional context; no exact entry found. USDA composite used as primary nutrient anchor.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — carrot juice and orange juice analogous estimates
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for a carrot-orange juice blend. Carrot juice has a published GI of approximately 43 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Orange juice has a published GI of approximately 50 (University of Sydney GI Database). A weighted average of a 50:50 blend yields approximately 46; however, the higher fibre contribution from carrot (1.33 g/100g) moderates absorption, and the conservative estimate of GI 43 is applied, consistent with the lower bound of the blend. GL per serving = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (43 x 27.99) / 100 = 12.0, rounded to 11.9 for 300 g serving (carbohydrate 9.33 g/100g x 3 = 27.99 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Free sugar concentration (6.67 g/100 g) in liquid form exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; juicing removes the fibre matrix that would otherwise moderate insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit carrot orange juice to no more than 100–150 ml per occasion and prefer whole carrots and whole oranges to retain fibre and reduce the free-sugar load; the naturally low sodium (13.33 mg/100 g) and beneficial potassium content are positive factors, but the concentrated free sugars independently elevate blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Juicing concentrates free sugars (6.67 g/100 g) and removes the fibre matrix of whole produce, accelerating glucose absorption and producing a glycaemic response equivalent to a sugar-sweetened beverage
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat carrot orange juice as a sugar-sweetened beverage equivalent; if consumed, limit to a maximum of 120 ml per occasion, pair with a protein or fat source to blunt the glucose spike, and preferably replace with whole carrots and whole oranges to benefit from fibre-modulated glucose absorption per ADA 2023 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and meaningful vitamin C (26.67 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene from carrot support a favourable cardiovascular nutrient profile; however, free sugar concentration in liquid form warrants portion control
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Carrot orange juice is low in saturated fat and trans fat and provides antioxidant vitamin C and pro-vitamin A (beta-carotene), which are cardioprotective; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 150 ml per day to avoid excess free-sugar intake, which is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA 2019 guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-carotene from carrot and vitamin C (26.67 mg/100 g) from orange are potent antioxidants that reduce oxidative stress and inflammatory mediators relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Carrot orange juice provides anti-inflammatory antioxidants (vitamin C and beta-carotene) that may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; limit portions to 150 ml to avoid excess free-sugar intake, which can promote low-grade inflammation at high doses, and prefer whole fruit and vegetables when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carrot orange juice is a blended fruit and vegetable beverage made by juicing fresh carrots and oranges, typically in approximately equal proportions. The resulting drink has a vibrant orange colour, a naturally sweet and slightly tangy flavour profile, and a smooth, thin consistency. It is commonly served chilled as a refreshing health drink in Singapore hawker centres, juice bars, and cafes, as well as across Southeast Asia and internationally. Per 100 g, the juice provides approximately 40 kcal, 0.67 g protein, 0.17 g fat, 9.33 g carbohydrate (of which 6.67 g are sugars), 1.33 g dietary fibre, 13.33 mg sodium, 20 mg calcium, 0.17 mg iron, and 26.67 mg vitamin C. The carrot component contributes beta-carotene (pro-vitamin A), which is a potent antioxidant, while the orange component contributes vitamin C and flavonoids. Despite its whole-food origins, juicing removes the fibre matrix of the intact produce, concentrating free sugars and accelerating glucose absorption. This makes carrot orange juice a moderate glycaemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension, where it should be treated similarly to a sugar-sweetened beverage and limited to 100–150 ml per occasion. The beverage is naturally halal, vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately 300 ml (one glass), providing around 120 kcal and 28 g carbohydrate per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in carrot orange juice?
Can diabetics drink carrot orange juice?
Is carrot orange juice good for blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of carrot orange juice?
How much vitamin C in carrot orange juice?
Carrot orange juice GI glycemic index
Is carrot orange juice suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a carrot-orange juice blend. Nutrient values are derived from a USDA SR Legacy composite of carrot juice and orange juice in a 50:50 ratio, which is consistent with the input record's per-100g profile. The input values are plausible and internally consistent. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for carrot juice and orange juice. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact blend ratio and preparation method are not confirmed by a single approved-source entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite of carrot juice (FDC ID 167771) and orange juice (FDC ID 169098) in an approximate 50:50 ratio; no single approved-source entry exists for this blend. Input values are plausible and retained. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed values for this specific carrot-orange juice blend are not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as a single composite entry, and introducing individual-component estimates without a confirmed blend ratio would risk unsupported precision: monounsaturated_fat_g (-1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1), potassium_mg (-1), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (-1), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (-1), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (-1), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (-1), vitamin_b6_mg (-1), vitamin_b12_mcg (-1), folate_mcg_dfe (-1), vitamin_d_mcg (-1), vitamin_e_mg (-1), phosphorus_mg (-1), magnesium_mg (-1), zinc_mg (-1), selenium_mcg (-1), water_g (-1). Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability of a single composite entry: USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/, accessed 2026-04-16) and HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero for a plant-based juice with no added fats; naturally zero for this food type. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage containing no animal-derived ingredients. 4. Cultural metadata in the input record incorrectly assigned primary_cuisine as 'Mexican' with ethnic_tags 'Mexican, Taco, Burrito' — these are clearly erroneous for a carrot-orange juice. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean / International' and region_of_origin to 'Singapore' consistent with the input's country_of_origin field. 5. GI estimated at 43 (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for carrot juice (GI ~43) and orange juice (GI ~50); GL per 300 g serving = 11.9. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 (fruit juice, liquid) used as closest_parent match; FoodEx2 A00LC (fruit and vegetable juices) used as closest_parent match; no SNOMED CT code identified. No ontology codes were invented. 7. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — check passed.
